column2: Question
Briefly describe the teams that will be involved in this initiative.
column3:
Our teams follow the Spotify model: squads composed of a Product Manager, an Engineering Manager, a QA Engineer, and a group of Software Engineers. Most of the SEs are Senior Backend developers, although we have also included some Senior Frontend developers in the course.

Cross-functional roles, such as Tech Leads, will also be participating in the course.

column2: What is your Backend stack?
What is your Database management stack?
What is your Cloud computing stack?
column3: All our services are dockerized in kubernetes, hosted in Google Cloud. We don't user BiQuery but Cloud SQL

column2: What is your Devops stack?
column3: GitLab Pipelines, ArgoCD, Helm, GKE (Kubernetes), Terraform, Grafana Cloud, Vault, Keycloak, Cloudflare, Ansible

column2: What is your Frontend stack?
column3: Angular and Nuxt. The main project is using Angular.

column2: What is your project management stack?
What is your mobile development stack?
What is your monitoring stack?
What is your QA stack?
What is your repository management system?
Do you work with defined sprints or iterations?
What is the duration of your sprints?
What day of the week does the sprint start?\*
What is the start date of your current (or next) sprint?
What is the start date of the following sprint (sprint +1)?
What is the start date of sprint +2?
Do you hold a formal planning session at the start of each sprint?
column3: Always, it's part of our process

column2: How long does a typical planning session last?
Who participates in sprint planning?
column3: Product Owner, Developers, QA/Testing, Scrum Master

column2: Is a clear sprint goal defined for each sprint?
Do you hold refinement (grooming) sessions before planning?
Stories/tasks arrive at planning with clear acceptance criteria
Is the team's actual capacity considered when planning? (PTO, tech debt, interruptions)
The backlog is prioritized and the team understands the priorities
Which ceremonies do you hold regularly?
How often do you hold retrospectives?
Are action items from retrospectives implemented in subsequent sprints?
Our planning sessions produce a realistic and achievable sprint plan
What percentage of the sprint backlog is typically completed?
What is the biggest obstacle in your sprint planning?
column3: Poor estimation, Interruptions/urgent requests, Unplanned tech debt

column2: Overall, how satisfied are you with your team's sprint planning process?
What would you change or improve about the current planning process?
column3: More accurate estimation. For some teams the meeting takes too long (3h).

column2: Is there anything else you'd like to share about how you plan your sprints?
column3: Sometimes the criteria of a task is not 100% accurate.

column2: On a scale from 1 to 5, How would you rate the overall maturity of the engineering organization?
What are the main technical constraints you are facing today?
column3: Development speed, Lack of reliable tests, High cognitive load / complexity, Inefficient use of AI, Code quality / production incidents

column2: How would you describe the current adoption of testing practices?
Unit tests
Integration tests
End-to-end tests
What is the biggest pain point when writing or maintaining tests today?
column3:
We have very complex use cases that transfer that same complexity to the tests.

For newcomers, these tests contain a lot of "magic" (helpers and such), which means getting up to speed with the ecosystem takes some time.

column2: Which testing anti-patterns do you observe most often?
column3: Flaky or fragile tests, Tests written after the code “for compliance”

column2: How would you describe CI feedback time today?
What currently hurts developer experience the most?
On a scale from 1 to 5, how much does CI/CD actually help enforce quality today?
Which AI tools are currently used in development workflows?
How integrated is AI in day-to-day development?
What are the main AI use cases today?
column3: Writing or improving tests, Refactoring, Code generation / autocomplete, Debugging, Documentation

column2: Which AI-related risks or anti-patterns concern you most?
column3: Incorrect outputs reaching production, Copy/paste without understanding, Inconsistent usage across teams

column2: Tooling adequacy
Workflow integration
Quality of AI-assisted output
Security / compliance awareness
Knowledge sharing and learning
Are there clear guidelines or agreements on AI usage today?
Would defining standards (IDE, assistants, workflows) be valuable at this stage?
How would you describe the team’s attitude toward changes like AI adoption or stronger testing practices?
Do you already see clear early adopters driving these practices?
What could realistically slow down or block adoption?
column3: Not seeing the value of AI adoption. Not as a game changer.

column2: What should be the top 2–3 priorities of this program?
column3:
Increase development speed and productivity.
Maintain high and consistent code quality standards.
Increase E2E test coverage and improve their maintainability.

column2: At the end of the program, what outcomes would make you consider it a success?
column3:
Reduction in task lead time and an increase in the number of features developed.
Reduction in lead time for bug fixes.
Fewer bugs introduced by AI than by humans.
